topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly aimed stone. Dr. Albright was leaving. The subject line was disappointingly neutral, but the words themselves felt like a personal slight. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated through my temples. For six months, every late-night session, every painstakingly polished paragraph, every agonizing revision had been calibrated around Dr. Albright’s specific, idiosyncratic feedback. Now, that calibration was dissolving. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet library cubicle. I couldn't believe this. After all the groundwork, the shared vision, the intellectual scaffolding we’d built together—it was just being discarded because of some arbitrary departmental reshuffle. I stood up, pacing the small space, my hands clenching and unclenching at my sides. "Unbelievable," I muttered to the empty room, the word tasting like ash.

[story 2]

I walked into the faculty lounge, expecting a casual chat, but the air felt thick and hostile. When I finally cornered Dr. Albright by the water cooler, my voice was unnaturally level, almost too calm. "So, you're actually leaving?" I watched his composure falter for a fleeting second—a flicker of something I couldn't quite decipher—before he offered a practiced, tight smile. My knuckles were white where I gripped my coffee mug. "After everything I’ve done for this project," I continued, my tone laced with a brittle sharpness, "this feels entirely unconsidered. It’s as if the value of the work we produced is somehow contingent on your continued presence. I need to understand the rationale behind this sudden departure." I waited, watching his carefully constructed facade begin to crack under the direct pressure of my scrutiny.

[story 3]

The announcement had circulated through the department like a slow-moving contagion, and I felt physically ill. I sat at my desk, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead seeming to amplify the rising tension in my chest. I picked up the printed memo, tracing the lines of the official notification with a shaking finger. It wasn't just a change in personnel; it was the dismantling of my entire academic trajectory. A slow, simmering heat began to build behind my eyes. I pushed the paper away, the sound a sharp rip in the silence. I started furiously typing, not to work, but to vent—a cascade of angry, precise sentences aimed at the very structure that had just betrayed me. Each keystroke was heavy, deliberate, a physical manifestation of the sheer unfairness of the situation.
